abstract: Although the green revolution adapted a handful of crops to homogeneous and high-input industrialized agriculture, much of the global population still relies on the local production of variable crop cultivars by low-input smallholder farms. This diversity of unhomogenized crops offers raw materials for genetic gain and cultivar improvement. Here, we constructed a 33-member pangenome reference and a diversity panel across 1,984 cultivars and landraces. Specifically, our analyses demonstrated multiple nested and deeply diverged structural variants in the domestication gene SHATTERING1. As proof of concept for pangenome-accelerated trait discovery, we connected biosynthetic gene cluster structural variation to phenotypic leaf concentration of the cyanogenic glucoside dhurrin. Combined, these approaches will accelerate breeding and trait discovery and provide a framework for similar applications in other crops.
notes:
## Key Findings

- 33-member sorghum pangenome + 1,984 cultivar/landrace diversity panel constructed.
- Multiple nested, deeply diverged SVs found in domestication gene SHATTERING1.
- Biosynthetic gene cluster SVs linked to dhurrin leaf concentration — proof-of-concept for pangenome-accelerated trait discovery.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Graph-based pangenome construction from diverse sorghum cultivars and landraces.
- SV-GWAS connecting pangenome structural variation to quantitative phenotypes.

title: Population-level structural variant characterization using pangenome graphs
abstract: Population-level structural variant (SV) profiling is crucial in the era of pangenomes. Here we present Swave, a sequence-to-image, deep learning-based method that accurately resolves both simple and complex SVs from assembly-derived pangenome graphs. Swave introduces 'projection waves' to summarize dotplot images capturing mapping patterns between reference and SV alleles. A recurrent neural network distinguishes true SV signals from background noise. When applied to healthy and rare-disease cohorts, Swave reveals complex and polymorphic SV patterns across human populations and identifies potentially pathogenic SVs, facilitating the creation of comprehensive population-level SV catalogs.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Swave: sequence-to-image deep learning method resolves simple and complex SVs from pangenome graphs.
- 'Projection waves' summarize dotplot images of mapping patterns between reference and SV alleles.
- RNN distinguishes true SV signals from background noise; applied to healthy and rare-disease cohorts.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Converts SV detection to an image classification problem using dotplot projections.
- Recurrent neural network applied to pangenome-derived sequence-to-image representations.

title: Predicting disease-specific histone modifications and functional effects of non-coding variants by leveraging DNA language models
abstract: The authors develop a novel large language model-based deep learning framework tailored for disease-contextual prediction of histone modifications and variant effects. Using Alzheimer's disease as a case study, they integrate epigenomic data from multiple patient samples to construct a comprehensive, disease-specific histone modification dataset. Their model incorporates a Mixture of Experts architecture to distinguish between disease and healthy epigenetic states, demonstrating robust performance and providing insights into disease-associated genetic variants and pathways.
notes:
## Key Findings

- LLM-based Mixture of Experts framework for disease-contextual histone modification prediction.
- Integrates patient epigenomic data to distinguish disease vs. healthy epigenetic states.
- Applied to variant effect prediction in a disease-specific context (Alzheimer's disease).

## Methods Worth Noting

- Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture applied to DNA language model outputs.
- Epigenomic dataset constructed from multiple patient samples for training.

title: Pangenome analysis reveals families of ubiquitin-ligase adaptors as key genomic divergence drivers that lead to hybrid incompatibility
abstract: This pangenome study analyzes populations from multiple species, revealing that genomic divergence is primarily driven by large interspecific unaligned regions. The research identifies specific families of ubiquitin-ligase adaptors as key drivers of genomic divergence, contributing to our understanding of hybrid incompatibility and genome evolution through a pangenomic lens.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Pangenome analysis reveals ubiquitin-ligase adaptor gene families as primary drivers of interspecific genomic divergence.
- Large interspecific unaligned regions are the dominant source of genomic divergence.
- Hybrid incompatibility linked to accumulation of these divergence-driving gene families.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Multi-species pangenome construction to identify unaligned/divergent regions.
- Comparative genomics across population samples from multiple species.

title: Automated interpretable artificial intelligence genomic prediction with AIGP
abstract: The paper introduces Artificial Intelligence Genomic Prediction (AIGP), an open-source, end-to-end toolkit designed for genomic prediction research. AIGP aims to provide both automated performance and interpretability, addressing the 'black box' nature of traditional deep learning models in genomics by incorporating architectures that allow for biological interpretation of the prediction process.
notes:
## Key Findings

- AIGP: open-source end-to-end toolkit for interpretable AI genomic prediction.
- Addresses the 'black box' problem in deep learning for genomics via interpretable architectures.
- Automated performance with biological interpretation of prediction.

## Methods Worth Noting

- End-to-end toolkit design for genomic prediction research.
- Incorporates architectures enabling biological interpretation alongside high prediction performance.

title: ADAR-GPT: Learning the language of the epitranscriptome
abstract: The study introduces ADAR-GPT, a framework that adapts GPT-class language models to the field of epitranscriptomics. By treating RNA sequences and their modifications as a biological language, the model learns to identify and classify A-to-I RNA editing sites with high accuracy across different tissues and species, demonstrating the power of language modeling in understanding complex biological sequences.
notes:
## Key Findings

- ADAR-GPT: GPT-class language model applied to epitranscriptomics for A-to-I RNA editing site classification.
- Treats RNA sequences + modifications as a biological language; high accuracy across tissues and species.
- Extends biological LM paradigm beyond DNA to RNA modifications.

## Methods Worth Noting

- GPT-class architecture adapted to RNA sequence + modification inputs.
- Trained and evaluated across multiple tissues and species for cross-context generalization.

title: Many roads lead to a plant cistrome: mapping and interpreting transcription factor binding in plants
abstract: This comprehensive review examines the current strategies and challenges in mapping the cistromes of plant species. It discusses high-throughput methods for identifying transcription factor binding sites and the computational challenges in interpreting these regulatory elements across diverse plant species, highlighting the importance of cistromics in crop improvement and plant biology.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Comprehensive review of strategies for mapping plant TF cistromes across species.
- Highlights computational challenges in interpreting regulatory elements across diverse plant genomes.
- Discusses implications for crop improvement and functional plant biology.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Review of high-throughput methods: ChIP-seq, DAP-seq, ATAC-seq, CUT&RUN in plant systems.
- Comparative cistromics across species to identify conserved regulatory elements.

title: GE-BiCross: A Hierarchical Bidirectional Cross-Attention Framework for Genotype-by-Environment Prediction in Maize
abstract: Genotype-by-environment interactions are central to crop adaptation and yield stability. We developed GE-BiCross, a hierarchical bidirectional cross-attention framework for maize prediction, which consistently outperformed machine learning and deep learning baselines across six agronomic traits using a large-scale dataset of 360,000 observations.
notes:
## Key Findings

- GE-BiCross: hierarchical bidirectional cross-attention framework for maize GxE prediction.
- Outperforms ML and DL baselines across 6 agronomic traits on 360,000 observations.
- Bidirectional attention captures complex genotype-environment interaction patterns.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Hierarchical bidirectional cross-attention architecture for joint genotype-environment modeling.
- Large-scale dataset of 360,000 observations across diverse environments.

title: HitAnno: Atlas-level cell type annotation based on scATAC-seq data via a hierarchical language model
abstract: HitAnno is a hierarchical language model for accurate and scalable cell type annotation in scATAC-seq data. It employs a two-level attention mechanism that captures accessibility profiles hierarchically, revealing co-accessibility patterns among peaks.
notes:
## Key Findings

- HitAnno: hierarchical language model for atlas-level cell type annotation from scATAC-seq.
- Two-level attention captures co-accessibility patterns among chromatin peaks.
- Scalable to atlas-level datasets.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Hierarchical language model with two-level attention for chromatin accessibility profiles.
- Applied to large-scale scATAC-seq datasets for cell type annotation.

title: A DNA foundation model predicts osteoporosis risk genes without proximity bias
abstract: Rosalind is a DNA foundation model fine-tuned on human genetic variation that predicts variant-gene regulatory relationships from sequence without relying on nearest-gene heuristics. It demonstrates high accuracy in osteoporosis risk gene prediction.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Rosalind: DNA foundation model fine-tuned on human genetic variation for variant-gene regulatory prediction.
- Predicts regulatory relationships from sequence alone, without nearest-gene proximity heuristics.
- High accuracy demonstrated for osteoporosis risk gene prediction.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Fine-tuning of DNA foundation model on human genetic variation data.
- Sequence-only prediction approach that bypasses proximity bias inherent in GWAS follow-up.

title: CDS-BART: A BART-Based Foundation Model for mRNA Sequence Analysis
abstract: Proposed CDS-BART is a foundation model for mRNA sequence analysis that integrates SentencePiece tokenization with BART training. It was pre-trained on mRNA data from nine taxonomic groups to learn codon usage and mRNA structure.
notes:
## Key Findings

- CDS-BART: BART-style foundation model pre-trained on mRNA data from nine taxonomic groups.
- Learns codon usage patterns and mRNA structural features across organisms.
- SentencePiece tokenization integrated with BART encoder-decoder training.

## Methods Worth Noting

- BART encoder-decoder architecture adapted for biological sequence modeling.
- Multi-taxon pre-training enables cross-species mRNA analysis.

title: FishMamba-1: A Linear-Complexity Foundation Model for Deciphering Polyploid Cyprinid Genomes
abstract: FishMamba is the first genomic foundation model tailored for polyploid genomes, built on the Mamba-2 architecture. It processes context windows of 32kb, enabling the modeling of distal regulatory patterns in expanded genomes.
notes:
## Key Findings

- FishMamba-1: first genomic foundation model specifically designed for polyploid genomes, built on Mamba-2.
- 32kb context window enables modeling of distal regulatory elements in expanded polyploid genomes.
- Linear-complexity architecture scales efficiently to large, repetitive polyploid genomes.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Mamba-2 state space model architecture for linear-complexity long-range sequence modeling.
- Applied to cyprinid fish polyploid genomes; directly translatable to plant polyploidy contexts.

title: Benchmarking DNA Foundation Models: Biological Blind Spots in Evo2 Variant-Effect Prediction
abstract: Evaluated Evo2 on controlled benchmarks for variant-effect prediction, identifying systematic blind spots in short-range biological signals and sensitivity to neutral contextual features, challenging claims of zero-shot pathogenicity prediction.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Systematic evaluation of Evo2 on controlled benchmarks for variant-effect prediction.
- Identifies blind spots in short-range biological signals; sensitive to neutral contextual features.
- Challenges zero-shot pathogenicity prediction claims for current DNA foundation models.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Controlled benchmark design to isolate specific capabilities and failure modes of DNA FMs.
- Systematic analysis of sensitivity to biological vs. neutral sequence context.

title: Uncovering genetic mechanisms underlying trait variation in switchgrass using explainable artificial intelligence
abstract: Integrated SNPs and RNA-seq data with machine learning models in switchgrass to predict trait plasticity. Explainable AI methods recovered canonical flowering regulators and revealed novel environment-specific candidate genes.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Integrated SNPs + RNA-seq with ML in switchgrass to predict trait plasticity.
- Explainable AI recovers canonical flowering regulators (e.g., photoperiod genes).
- Reveals novel environment-specific candidate genes not identified by traditional GWAS.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Multi-omic integration: SNP genotypes + transcriptome data for ML-based trait prediction.
- Explainable AI (XAI) applied to identify causal regulatory candidates.

title: CollapsedChrom: resolving the assembly of collapsed chromosomal segments in polyploid genomes of the model grass genus Brachypodium
abstract: Developed a depth-sensitive curation strategy (CollapsedChrom) to detect and rescue collapsed regions in polyploid Brachypodium genomes, capturing the full genomic architecture of these perennial grasses.
notes:
## Key Findings

- CollapsedChrom: depth-sensitive strategy to detect and rescue collapsed chromosomal segments in polyploid plant genomes.
- Applied to Brachypodium polyploid species to capture full genomic architecture.
- Rescues previously missed genomic sequences in repetitive/collapsed regions.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Sequencing depth analysis to identify collapsed vs. properly assembled regions.
- Curation pipeline that recovers collapsed segments and restores genomic completeness.

title: The Genomic Legacy of Ancient Polyploidy in Crop Domestication
abstract: Tested whether paleologs from ancient whole-genome duplications are enriched in domestication gene lists across 22 crop species. Found that purifying selection on copy number does not preclude functional selection.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Tests enrichment of ancient WGD paleologs in domestication gene lists across 22 crop species.
- Purifying selection on copy number does not preclude subsequent functional selection on retained duplicates.
- Links ancient polyploidy evolutionary history directly to crop domestication.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Cross-species comparative genomics across 22 domesticated crop species.
- Integration of WGD paleolog databases with crop domestication gene lists.

title: SNPgen: Phenotype-Supervised Genotype Representation and Synthetic Data Generation via Latent Diffusion
abstract: SNPgen is a two-stage conditional latent diffusion framework for generating phenotype-supervised synthetic genotypes. It matches real-data predictive performance for complex diseases while ensuring privacy preservation.
notes:
## Key Findings

- SNPgen: two-stage conditional latent diffusion for phenotype-supervised synthetic genotype generation.
- Matches real-data predictive performance for complex diseases while being privacy-preserving.
- Enables synthetic genomics data generation for downstream ML training.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Two-stage: latent encoding of genotypes + conditional diffusion for phenotype-guided generation.
- Privacy-preserving by design; synthetic data matches statistical properties of real genotypes.

title: Continuous Diffusion Transformers for Designing Synthetic Regulatory Elements
abstract: Presented a Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for generating 200bp cell-type-specific regulatory DNA sequences. It improves predicted regulatory activity by 38x using Enformer-based reinforcement learning.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Diffusion Transformer (DiT) generates 200bp cell-type-specific regulatory DNA sequences.
- 38× improvement in predicted regulatory activity vs. baseline using Enformer-based RL fine-tuning.
- Continuous diffusion in sequence space enables high-quality biological sequence design.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Continuous Diffusion Transformer architecture adapted for discrete DNA sequence space.
- Enformer-based reinforcement learning reward signal guides regulatory activity optimization.

title: Discovery of a Hematopoietic Manifold in scGPT Yields a Method for Extracting Performant Algorithms from Biological Foundation Model Internals
abstract: Discovery of a compact algorithm extracted from the scGPT foundation model internals using mechanistic interpretability. The extracted operator resolves into explicit gene programs without retraining.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Mechanistic interpretability applied to scGPT reveals a compact hematopoietic manifold in model internals.
- Extracted algorithm resolves into explicit gene programs without any retraining.
- Demonstrates that useful biology can be extracted directly from foundation model weights.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Mechanistic interpretability techniques (activation patching, probing) applied to scGPT.
- Algorithm extraction from model internals without fine-tuning or retraining.

title: Transposable Element Dynamics Drive Genome Size Variation along Environmental Gradients in Hordeum
abstract: A substantial variation in genome size has been observed among individuals of the same species. Theory predicts that increased genome size may confer an advantage in populations with small effective size. However, contradictory evidence for the correlation with environmental variation, and limited understanding of the underlying genetic mechanisms, has cast doubts on the adaptive role of genome size variation. To address this, we studied two Hordeum species which were collected at the same sites along a wide range of environments but differ in life habits (annual/perennial) and mating strategy (self/outcrossing). We detected substantial genome size variation, with differences of up to 10% in both species. While the mating system influenced the distribution of variation within populations, it did not alter the overall range of genome sizes. Drought emerged as the primary environmental factor associated with larger genomes, with past transposable elements (TE) bursts identified as the potential source of genome expansion. Genome-wide association analyses revealed that TE silencing is central to genome size regulation, with selection maintaining smaller genomes among individuals with higher fitness in favorable habitats. Under stressful conditions, silencing becomes less efficient, allowing larger genomes to persist and thereby increasing the reservoir of genetic variation available for selection. Together, these findings provide an integrative view of genome size dynamics in natural populations and underscore its exaptive role in maintenance of genetic variation under environmental stress.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Genome size varies up to 10% within *Hordeum* species, primarily driven by TE burst activity.
- Drought is the main environmental factor correlated with larger genome size.
- GWAS reveals TE silencing machinery as the central regulator of genome size; selection favors smaller genomes in favorable habitats but relaxes under stress, allowing TE-expanded genomes to persist.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Comparative study of annual/perennial and self/outcrossing *Hordeum* pairs sampled at the same sites for controlled environmental comparison.
- Genome-wide association study (GWAS) linking genome size to TE silencing gene variants.
- TE burst timing inferred from genomic analysis to link historical TE activity to current genome expansion.

title: Signatures of Soft Selective Sweeps Predominate in the Yellow Fever Mosquito Aedes aegypti
abstract: The Aedes aegypti mosquito is a vector for human arboviruses and zoonotic diseases and therefore poses a serious threat to public health. Understanding how Ae. aegypti adapts to environmental pressures-such as insecticides-is critical for developing effective mitigation strategies. However, most traditional methods for detecting recent positive selection search for signatures of classic 'hard' selective sweeps, and to date no studies have examined soft sweeps in Ae. aegypti. This is a significant limitation as this is vital information for understanding the pace of adaptation-populations that can immediately respond to new selective pressures are expected to adapt more often via standing variation or recurrent adaptive mutations (both of which may produce soft sweeps) than via de novo mutations (which produces hard sweeps). To this end, we used a machine learning method capable of detecting hard and soft sweeps to investigate positive selection in Ae. aegypti population samples from Africa and the Americas. Our results reveal that soft sweeps are significantly more common than hard sweeps, which may imply that this species can respond quickly to environmental stressors. This is a particularly concerning finding for vector control methods that aim to eradicate Ae. aegypti using insecticides. We highlight genes under selection that include both well-characterized and putatively novel insecticide resistance genes. These findings underscore the importance of using methods capable of detecting and distinguishing hard and soft sweeps, implicate soft sweeps as a major selective mode in Ae. aegypti, and highlight genes that may aid in the control of Ae. aegypti populations.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Soft selective sweeps (from standing variation or recurrent mutations) are significantly more common than hard sweeps in *Ae. aegypti*, suggesting this mosquito adaptss rapidly to new pressures like insecticides.
- ML method successfully distinguishes hard vs. soft sweep signatures genome-wide, identifying both known and novel candidate insecticide resistance genes.
- Findings have direct implications for vector control: insecticide eradication strategies may be undermined by soft-sweep-mediated rapid resistance evolution.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Used a machine learning classifier capable of distinguishing hard sweeps, soft sweeps, and neutral regions from population genomic data.
- Applied to population samples from Africa and the Americas to assess geographic variation in sweep mode.
- Methodological approach is broadly applicable for population genomics studies in any species, including crop plants.

title: Conservation of regulatory elements with highly diverged sequences across large evolutionary distances
abstract: Developmental gene expression is a remarkably conserved process, yet most cis-regulatory elements (CREs) lack sequence conservation, especially at larger evolutionary distances. Some evidence suggests that CREs at the same genomic position remain functionally conserved independent of sequence conservation. However, the extent of such positional conservation remains unclear. Here, we profiled the regulatory genome in mouse and chicken embryonic hearts at equivalent developmental stages and found that most CREs lack sequence conservation. To identify positionally conserved CREs, we introduced the synteny-based algorithm interspecies point projection, which identifies up to fivefold more orthologs than alignment-based approaches. We termed positionally conserved orthologs 'indirectly conserved' and showed that they exhibited chromatin signatures and sequence composition similar to sequence-conserved CREs but greater shuffling of transcription factor binding sites between orthologs. Finally, we validated indirectly conserved chicken enhancers using in vivo reporter assays in mouse. By overcoming alignment-based limitations, we revealed widespread functional conservation of sequence-divergent CREs.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Most cis-regulatory elements (CREs) lack sequence conservation across large evolutionary distances, yet developmental gene expression remains conserved.
- A novel synteny-based algorithm called 'interspecies point projection' identifies up to 5x more orthologous CREs than alignment-based approaches.
- Positionally conserved ('indirectly conserved') CREs share chromatin signatures with sequence-conserved CREs but show greater shuffling of transcription factor binding sites.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Profiling of regulatory genome in mouse and chicken embryonic hearts at equivalent developmental stages.
- Synteny-based interspecies point projection algorithm for identifying positionally conserved CRE orthologs.
- In vivo reporter assays in mouse to validate predicted conserved chicken enhancers.

title: The human and non-human primate developmental GTEx projects
abstract: Many human diseases are the result of early developmental defects. As most paediatric diseases and disorders are rare, children are critically underrepresented in research. Functional genomics studies primarily rely on adult tissues and lack critical cell states in specific developmental windows. In parallel, little is known about the conservation of developmental programmes across non-human primate (NHP) species, with implications for human evolution. Here we introduce the developmental Genotype-Tissue Expression (dGTEx) projects, which span humans and NHPs and aim to integrate gene expression, regulation and genetics data across development and species. The dGTEx cohort will consist of 74 tissue sites across 120 human donors from birth to adulthood, and developmentally matched NHP age groups, with additional prenatal and adult animals, with 126 rhesus macaques (Macaca mulatta) and 72 common marmosets (Callithrix jacchus). The data will comprise whole-genome sequencing, extensive bulk, single-cell and spatial gene expression profiles, and chromatin accessibility data across tissues and development. Through community engagement and donor diversity, the human dGTEx study seeks to address disparities in genomic research. Thus, dGTEx will provide a reference human and NHP dataset and tissue bank, enabling research into developmental changes in expression and gene regulation, childhood disorders and the effect of genetic variation on development.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Introduces the developmental GTEx (dGTEx) project spanning humans and non-human primates (NHPs) to study developmental gene expression and regulation.
- Cohort includes 120 human donors (birth to adulthood) across 74 tissue sites, plus 126 rhesus macaques and 72 common marmosets at matched developmental stages.
- Will integrate WGS, bulk/single-cell/spatial transcriptomics, and chromatin accessibility data across development.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Multi-species comparative approach: human, rhesus macaque, and common marmoset developmental cohorts.
- Combined multimodal genomics: WGS, bulk RNA-seq, scRNA-seq, spatial transcriptomics, and ATAC-seq.
- Community engagement strategy to address disparities in genomic research representation.

title: Evolutionary transcriptomics unveils rapid changes of gene expression patterns in flowering plants
abstract: The evolutionary history of angiosperms (flowering plants) is characterized by a highly accelerated rate of diversification. Although a number of hypotheses have been proposed to explain the ecological success and rapid rise of angiosperms, the molecular mechanisms underlying their speciation are only partially understood. In this study, we analyzed the developmental transcriptomes of seven angiosperm species spanning 160 million years of evolution. We demonstrate that angiosperm protein-coding gene expression patterns diverged rapidly. Particularly high rates of expression changes were found for genes that are involved in the response to endogenous and environmental stimuli, promoting broad ecological tolerances, adaptive evolution, and speciation. This study highlights how ecological and evolutionary dynamics are highly interrelated, shows that the rates of expression divergence differ from those previously described for mammals, and provides a comprehensive resource to perform cross-kingdom comparative studies of transcriptome evolution.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Analyzed developmental transcriptomes of 7 angiosperm species spanning 160 million years of evolution, revealing rapid divergence of gene expression patterns.
- Genes involved in response to endogenous and environmental stimuli show the highest rates of expression change, linking ecological adaptation to speciation.
- Rates of expression divergence in angiosperms differ markedly from those described in mammals, highlighting kingdom-level differences.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Comparative developmental transcriptomics across 7 angiosperm species chosen to span 160 million years of evolutionary history.
- Cross-kingdom comparison with existing mammalian transcriptome evolution data.
- Provides a public resource for comparative transcriptome evolution studies.

title: Genomic Next-Token Predictors are In-Context Learners
abstract: In-context learning (ICL) -- the capacity of a model to infer and apply abstract patterns from examples provided within its input -- has been extensively studied in large language models trained for next-token prediction on human text. In fact, prior work often attributes this emergent behavior to distinctive statistical properties in human language. This raises a fundamental question: can ICL arise organically in other sequence domains purely through large-scale predictive training? To explore this, we turn to genomic sequences, an alternative symbolic domain rich in statistical structure. Specifically, we study the Evo2 genomic model, trained predominantly on next-nucleotide (A/T/C/G) prediction, at a scale comparable to mid-sized LLMs. We develop a controlled experimental framework comprising symbolic reasoning tasks instantiated in both linguistic and genomic forms, enabling direct comparison of ICL across genomic and linguistic models. Our results show that genomic models, like their linguistic counterparts, exhibit log-linear gains in pattern induction as the number of in-context demonstrations increases. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first evidence of organically emergent ICL in genomic sequences, supporting the hypothesis that ICL arises as a consequence of large-scale predictive modeling over rich data. These findings extend emergent meta-learning beyond language, pointing toward a unified, modality-agnostic view of in-context learning.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Evo2 genomic foundation model exhibits in-context learning (ICL) capabilities analogous to language models, showing log-linear gains with more in-context demonstrations
- First evidence of organically emergent ICL in genomic sequences, supporting the hypothesis that ICL is a general property of large-scale predictive models
- Results suggest a unified, modality-agnostic view of in-context learning that extends beyond language to DNA sequences

## Methods Worth Noting

- Controlled experimental framework with symbolic reasoning tasks instantiated in both linguistic and genomic forms for direct comparison
- Evaluation of the Evo2 model (trained on next-nucleotide A/T/C/G prediction) at scale comparable to mid-sized LLMs

title: BioWorldModel: A Multi-Kingdom Trajectory Architecture for Genomic Prediction with Evolutionary Curriculum Learning
abstract: Genomic prediction models are trained on single species and ignore temporal dynamics -- assumptions that limit their biological scope. Here I present BioWorldModel, a unified architecture that predicts multi-trait phenotypic distributions across fungi, plants, and animals with a single set of parameters. The model introduces a scalable genotype encoder with organism-conditioned attention pooling, a four-channel biological memory system, and a Gaussian output head with diagonal variance parameterization. Trained jointly on five organisms spanning three kingdoms (S. cerevisiae, A. thaliana, D. melanogaster, O. sativa, Z. mays; 641 traits total), the model achieves organism-averaged R-squared = 0.821 (trait-weighted R-squared = 0.413) and substantially outperforms GBLUP, BayesB, Lasso, and Random Forest baselines trained per organism. These results demonstrate that genotype-to-phenotype mapping follows shared principles across kingdoms that a single model can exploit.
notes:
## Key Findings

- A single unified model predicts phenotypes across fungi, plants, and animals (5 organisms, 3 kingdoms, 641 traits) with organism-averaged R² = 0.821
- Substantially outperforms GBLUP, BayesB, Lasso, and Random Forest baselines that are trained per organism
- Demonstrates that genotype-to-phenotype mapping follows shared principles across kingdoms

## Methods Worth Noting

- Scalable genotype encoder with organism-conditioned attention pooling
- Four-channel biological memory system and Gaussian output head with diagonal variance parameterization
- Evolutionary curriculum learning for multi-kingdom training

title: Breeding ideotype maize with enhanced yield through genomics-guided pyramiding of favorable alleles
abstract: Breeding ideotype maize for high-density planting is crucial for increasing yield but is hindered by a limited understanding of plant architecture regulation. Here we uncover a prominent role for the accumulation of homozygous favorable alleles in genes regulating four principal shoot traits constituting a maize ideotype, and we identify and functionally validate eight architecture-regulating genes. Guided by genomics, we selected the elite hybrid Yufeng303 as a chassis to develop five improved parental lines by pyramiding favorable alleles of these eight genes together with genome-wide loci associated with these traits. These improved parental lines led to the creation of four new hybrids better suited for high-density planting, achieving 4.1–9.2% higher plot yields than Yufeng303 across eight environments. This study exemplifies the power of genomics-guided allele pyramiding in enhancing crop performance.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Identified and validated eight architecture-regulating genes controlling four principal shoot traits in maize ideotype
- Pyramiding favorable alleles of these genes created hybrids achieving 4.1–9.2% higher plot yields across eight environments
- Demonstrates practical genomics-guided breeding strategy for high-density planting maize improvement

## Methods Worth Noting

- Genomics-guided allele pyramiding approach combining GWAS-identified loci with functional validation
- Used elite hybrid Yufeng303 as chassis for developing improved parental lines

title: A genetic module boosts grain yield and nitrogen use efficiency by improving nitrate transport in maize
abstract: Although nitrogen fertilizer use has boosted crop yields, excessive application diminishes crop nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) and causes environmental problems. Therefore, increasing crop NUE is urgently needed for agricultural sustainability. Through a genome-wide association study, we identified a locus, NCR1 (Nitrate Concentration Regulator 1), that correlates with nitrate concentrations in maize root xylem. NCR1 encodes a MYB transcription factor that positively regulates the transcription of nitrate transporter NRT2.3 expressed predominantly in root xylem parenchyma cells. The NCR1–NRT2.3 transcription module responds to external nitrogen and controls nitrate translocation from roots to shoots. The superior NCR1−In allele with a 123-bp promoter deletion has decreased in frequency as nitrogen fertilizer use in China has increased. Overexpression of NCR1 or NRT2.3 significantly enhances grain yield and NUE in maize.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Identified NCR1 (MYB transcription factor) that regulates nitrate transporter NRT2.3, controlling nitrate translocation from roots to shoots in maize
- The superior NCR1−In allele (123-bp promoter deletion) has decreased in frequency with increased nitrogen fertilizer use in China
- Overexpression of NCR1 or NRT2.3 significantly enhances both grain yield and nitrogen use efficiency

## Methods Worth Noting

- GWAS on nitrate concentrations in maize root xylem to identify NCR1 locus
- Functional validation through overexpression and characterization of NCR1–NRT2.3 transcription module

title: Allelic variation at a single locus distinguishes spring and winter faba beans
abstract: Winter faba beans exhibit significant yield advantages over spring cultivars and hold promise for enhancing local protein production and agricultural sustainability. However, the threat of winter kill limits wider cultivation, and the genetics of faba bean winter hardiness remain unresolved. Here we develop a greatly improved faba bean reference genome and combine this with resequencing and phenotyping of winter and spring accessions to identify genetic determinants of winter hardiness. Genome-wide association analysis of frost tolerance traits identifies a major winter hardiness locus, the most strongly associated variant of which explains the vast majority of phenotypic variation and accurately differentiates between winter and spring types. Furthermore, we identify additional signals within the winter faba bean gene pool that could lead to further improvement of winter hardiness.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Developed a greatly improved faba bean reference genome
- Identified a major winter hardiness locus that explains the vast majority of phenotypic variation between winter and spring types
- Found additional genetic signals within winter faba beans for further hardiness improvement

## Methods Worth Noting

- Combined improved reference genome with resequencing and phenotyping of winter/spring accessions
- GWAS of frost tolerance traits to map the major winter hardiness locus

title: Single-Platform Nanopore Sequencing Enables Diploid Telomere-to-Telomere Genome Assembly and Haplotype-Resolved 3D Chromatin Maps
abstract: Telomere-to-telomere (T2T) genome assembly has transformed human genomics by resolving centromeres, segmental duplications, and other previously inaccessible regions. However, most diploid T2T assemblies rely on the combination of multi-platform sequencing strategies including short read genome sequencing, PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore ultra-long reads, and chromatin conformation capture data (Hi-C), limiting both scalability and accessibility. Here, we present a streamlined Nanopore-only workflow for diploid human T2T assembly using three ultra-long and one Pore-C PromethION flow cell per individual. Across 23 genetically diverse individuals, we generated 360 gapless chromosomes and 446 near-complete T2T scaffolds, achieving median consensus accuracy of QV50 without Duplex sequencing or hybrid polishing. Assembly continuity, gene completeness, and structural variant detection were comparable to multi-platform Human Pangenome Reference Consortium assemblies. Pore-C data enabled chromosome-scale haplotype phasing without parental information and supported generation of haplotype-resolved chromatin contact maps. Integrated methylation and 3D genome analyses revealed allele-specific chromatin organization at imprinted loci and clear signatures of X-chromosome inactivation. Our openly accessible dataset expands public T2T resources and demonstrates that reference-grade diploid assemblies, phased methylomes, and 3D genome maps can be derived from a single sequencing platform. This approach reduces technical barriers and supports scalable population and functional genomics in the T2T era.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Single-platform Nanopore-only workflow achieves diploid T2T assembly across 23 diverse individuals, generating 360 gapless chromosomes at QV50 median accuracy
- Pore-C data alone enables chromosome-scale haplotype phasing without parental genomes, comparable to HPRC multi-platform assemblies
- Integrated methylation and Pore-C 3D genome maps reveal allele-specific chromatin organization at imprinted loci and X-chromosome inactivation signatures

## Methods Worth Noting

- Three ultra-long + one Pore-C PromethION flow cells per individual as the complete sequencing strategy
- Haplotype phasing via Pore-C chromatin contact data without Hi-C or parental short reads
- QV50 consensus accuracy achieved without Duplex sequencing or hybrid polishing

title: Chromosome-level genome sequence of the C4 grass Themeda triandra reveals karyotype orthology with sorghum and genetic variation in accessions adapted to diverse environments
abstract: Themeda triandra (family Andropogoneae) is a pan-continental grass occurring throughout Australia. Recognising its remarkable adaptive capacity, we assembled the nuclear genome sequence of a diploid accession from eastern Australia, finding all ten chromosomes of the 755 Mb assembly highly syntenic with those of Sorghum bicolor. Genotyping and cytometry of range-wide accessions suggested several historical radiation events, with polyploids dominating in arid regions, apart from a notable diploid lineage from the arid north-west of Australia (PAN). A detailed comparison of PAN with two diploids from temperate south-eastern Australia through whole-genome resequencing revealed extensive copy number variation and polymorphism, with changes in genes for heat-shock proteins and flowering regulation reflecting their environmental origin. Exploration of the rich genetic diversity in T. triandra with respect to environmental adaptation is expected to benefit grassland management programs and enable introgression of novel genes into Andropogoneae crops for climate resilience.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Chromosome-level 755 Mb genome assembly of Themeda triandra (Andropogoneae) shows high synteny with Sorghum bicolor across all 10 chromosomes
- Range-wide genotyping reveals polyploids dominate in arid regions while a distinct diploid lineage (PAN) persists in arid north-west Australia
- Whole-genome resequencing identifies copy number variation and polymorphisms in heat-shock protein and flowering genes reflecting environmental adaptation

## Methods Worth Noting

- PacBio HiFi, Oxford Nanopore, and 10x Genomics linked reads for chromosome-scale assembly
- Cytometry and genotyping for population-level ploidy characterization
- Comparative genomics with Sorghum bicolor for synteny and karyotype orthology analysis

title: Evolutionary and Deep Learning Models Highlight Deleterious Mutations Behind the History of Sugar Beet Breeding
abstract: Sugar beet (Beta vulgaris ssp. vulgaris) is a major global source of sucrose. Domestication and a history of breeding have shaped the distribution of deleterious mutations across the genome. Accurately resolving these variants offers a compelling avenue to accelerate crop improvement through breeding or genetic engineering. We combine three scales of evolutionary time: protein conservation defined by conservation across all living organisms (SIFT), angiosperm-wide DNA language deep learning model (PlantCaduceus), and fine-scale evolutionary rates from Amaranthaceae plant family sequence alignments to define a high-confidence set of ~2k deleterious variants enriched for alleles under negative selection. We then evaluate these deleterious mutations across a multi-study panel of more than 1,900 whole-genome sequenced wild and cultivated beet accessions. We found that domesticated beet accessions exhibit higher deleterious load relative to wild maritima, yet sugar beet carries significantly fewer deleterious variants than other cultivated beet crops. Historical trends show a decline in genetic load across more than a century of sugar beet entries in the U.S. NPGS, reflecting sustained purging during modern breeding. This combinatorial method of leveraging evolutionary conservation reveals how deleterious mutations have shaped sugar beet breeding history and provides potential targets for future selection, causal-variant discovery, and precision genome engineering.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Combines SIFT, PlantCaduceus (angiosperm-wide DNA language model), and family-level evolutionary rates to identify ~2k high-confidence deleterious variants under negative selection in sugar beet
- Domesticated beet shows higher deleterious load than wild maritima, but sugar beet specifically has fewer deleterious variants than other cultivated beet crops
- Over a century of U.S. NPGS breeding history shows a measurable decline in genetic load, consistent with purging during modern selection

## Methods Worth Noting

- Multi-scale approach: protein-level (SIFT), DNA LM-level (PlantCaduceus), and clade-level (Amaranthaceae alignments) conservation combined
- Panel of >1,900 whole-genome sequenced wild and cultivated beet accessions for population-level evaluation
- PlantCaduceus used as an angiosperm-wide DNA language model for scoring variant deleteriousness

title: Chromosome-scale genome of the woody oilseed crop sacha inchi elucidates the molecular basis of alpha-linolenic acid biosynthesis and triacylglycerol accumulation in seeds
abstract: Sacha inchi (Plukenetia volubilis L.) is an emerging woody oilseed crop prized for its high alpha-linolenic acid (ALA) content. Despite its nutritional and economic value, the lack of high-quality genomic resources has hindered genetic improvement and the elucidation of its unique polyunsaturated fatty acid and lipid biosynthetic pathways. In this study, we report a high-quality, chromosome-scale genome assembly of sacha inchi with a total length of 710.62 Mb, integrated from Illumina, PacBio, and chromosome conformation capture (Hi-C) technology. The genome harbors 37,570 protein-coding genes, and 379.86 Mb (53.45%) of repetitive sequences. Phylogenomic analysis reveals that sacha inchi diverged from its closest relative Ricinus communis, approximately 36.2 million years ago. Comparative genomics indicates that sacha inchi experienced only ancient whole genome duplication events. To elucidate the mechanisms governing ALA biosynthesis and triacylglycerol (TAG) accumulation in sacha inchi seeds, we performed temporal transcriptome profiling across six seed development stages. Our findings demonstrate that high TAG content is primarily driven by the sustained expression of biosynthetic genes and low activity of degradation genes during mid-to-late seed development. Notably, while genes encoding stearoyl-ACP desaturases (SADs) maintain the precursor pool, the expression of genes encoding fatty-acid desaturase 2 (FAD2) and fatty-acid desaturase 3 (FAD3) is positively correlated with the final accumulation of C18:2 and C18:3 fatty acids. We also identified lncRNAs as potential epigenetic regulators of these key pathways. This high-quality genome provides a critical foundation for elucidating the molecular mechanisms of seed growth and development in sacha inchi.
notes:
## Key Findings

- High-quality 710.62 Mb chromosome-scale genome of sacha inchi with 37,570 protein-coding genes; 53.45% repetitive sequences
- Diverged from Ricinus communis ~36.2 Mya; experienced only ancient WGD events (no recent polyploidy)
- FAD2 and FAD3 expression strongly correlates with high ALA (C18:3) accumulation; lncRNAs implicated as epigenetic regulators of fatty acid pathways

## Methods Worth Noting

- Hybrid assembly combining Illumina, PacBio long reads, and Hi-C chromatin conformation capture
- Temporal transcriptome profiling across 6 seed development stages to link gene expression to lipid accumulation dynamics
- Phylogenomic analysis for divergence time estimation and WGD history

title: Weather Characterization for Optimizing Genomic Prediction in Miscanthus sacchariflorus
abstract: Environmental factors affect crop growth and development thus their consideration across sites and years become essential for genotypic evaluation. Genomic selection (GS) has been broadly implemented to accelerate breeding cycles by skipping field evaluations thus allowing early identification of outperforming genotypes. In this study, 7,740 phenotypic records corresponding to 516 Miscanthus sacchariflorus genotypes evaluated in five locations across three years were considered for analysis. Additionally, environmental data on six weather covariates was implemented to characterize similarities between locations. Different sets of locations of variable sizes were used for model calibration based on two cross-validations (CV00 and CV0) schemes leaving out one location at a time. Predictive ability across locations of the best model varied between 0.45 and 0.90 for both schemes. These results were compared to associate predictive ability in function of weather patterns between training and testing sets to allow model's calibration optimization. We found it is feasible to optimize resource allocation by considering environmentally correlated sets. In most cases, the information from only one and, at most, two locations were enough to deliver better results than using all four locations, reducing training sets by up to 75%.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Weather covariate-informed genomic selection in Miscanthus sacchariflorus (516 genotypes, 5 locations, 3 years) achieves predictive ability of 0.45–0.90 across locations
- Environmentally correlated training sets of just 1–2 locations can outperform models trained on all 4 locations, reducing data needs by up to 75%
- Weather similarity between training and test environments is a practical guide for optimizing genomic selection model calibration in perennial grasses

## Methods Worth Noting

- Two cross-validation schemes (CV00 and CV0) designed for leave-one-location-out evaluation
- Six weather covariates used to quantify environmental similarity between field locations
- Genomic selection framework applied to bioenergy perennial crop Miscanthus sacchariflorus

title: Position-dependent variant effects reveal importance of context in genomic regulation
abstract: Gene expression is governed by the DNA sequence, which is read out through complex interactions between transcription factors (TFs), co-activators, and chromatin. Massively Parallel Reporter Assays (MPRAs) provide a high-throughput framework for functionally characterizing how regulatory DNA sequences impact the expression of a model gene. MPRAs have also proven to be useful for measuring the effects of genetic variation, where each allele is typically tested in the center of ~200 bp of genomic context cloned into the MPRA; but the impact of variant position and local context remains largely unexplored. In this study, we systematically investigate how shifting the position of a variant within an MPRA probe influences its regulatory activity using models that predict expression in MPRAs from DNA sequence. We find that while the direction of variant effects is usually preserved across positions, the magnitude of expression changes can vary substantially depending on where the variant is placed within the construct. This positional bias appears to be largely explained by the strong position-dependent activity of TFs whose binding the variants perturb. In a subset of cases, interactions consistent with cooperativity between TFs also contributes to position-specific effects. ~1% of variants appear to disrupt RNA polymerase III (Pol III) promoters within Alu elements, resulting in position-specificity because both A and B boxes are required for function and exclusion of either motif due to window shifts disrupts the variants effects. However, we saw little evidence to support the hypothesis that the positional dependence of variant effects resulted from redundancy of motifs. Overall, our study demonstrates the complexity of cis-regulatory grammar and how it can confound the interpretation of regulatory variants.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Variant effect direction is generally preserved across positions within MPRA probes, but magnitude varies substantially—largely driven by position-dependent TF binding activity
- ~1% of variants disrupt Pol III promoters in Alu elements (requiring both A and B boxes), creating strong position-specific effects in MPRAs
- TF cooperativity contributes to position-specific effects in a subset of cases; motif redundancy is not a major driver of positional dependence

## Methods Worth Noting

- MPRA combined with sequence-to-expression deep learning models to systematically test variant effects at multiple positions
- Sequence models used to dissect TF binding and positional activity without exhaustive wet-lab experiments
- Framework reveals confounders in standard MPRA design (center-placement assumption) relevant to variant effect prediction benchmarks

title: Enhancing Genomic Prediction Models in Miscanthus Populations by Incorporating the Genotype-by-Environment Interaction
abstract: Giant Miscanthus giganteus (Mxg) is one of the most promising perennial crops to generate biomass feedstock for bioenergy and biobased products. It is derived from the natural inter-species hybridization of Miscanthus sacchariflorus (Msa) and Miscanthus sinensis (Msi) species, thus population improvement within these species is crucial. Genomic selection (GS) is an attractive option to accelerate breeding of perennial grasses, such as Miscanthus, which requires up to three years of evaluation to produce reliable phenotypic data. Hence, genotypes are observed in multiple years and locations causing inconsistent response patterns from one year to the next, between location, and/or location-by-year combinations. These inconsistencies are known as the genotype-by-environment interaction effect (GxE). Although GS has been successfully implemented in multiple annual crops where straightforward cross-validation schemes exist to assess the levels of predictive ability that can be reached, for perennial crops new cross-validation schemes will help avoid data contamination. Here, we propose a series of cross-validation schemes to evaluate model performance for perennial crops. We perform a case study by analyzing one panel of each species (516 genotypes of Msa, 280 genotypes of Msi) scored for biomass yield at different locations around the world over several years. The results of the different cross-validation schemes provide insights about the usefulness of GS to accelerate the breeding process of Miscanthus species. In addition, leveraging the GxE effects of different types significantly increases predictive ability (up to 10% in Msa and 30% for Msi) compared to the conventional approaches based on main effects only.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Incorporating GxE effects into genomic selection models improves predictive ability by up to 10% in M. sacchariflorus and up to 30% in M. sinensis versus main-effects-only models
- Novel cross-validation schemes for perennial crops (Miscanthus) designed to avoid data contamination from multi-year, multi-location phenotyping
- Case study on 516 Msa and 280 Msi genotypes for biomass yield across global locations demonstrates GxE is a critical component for GS in perennial grasses

## Methods Worth Noting

- New cross-validation framework specifically addressing temporal and spatial confounding in perennial crop GS evaluation
- GxE partitioned into different interaction types (year, location, year-by-location) and incorporated into genomic prediction models
- Companion paper to Shaik et al. 2026 (weather characterization) using the same Miscanthus panel

title: A Phylogenetic Approach to Genomic Language Modeling
abstract: Genomic language models (gLMs) have shown mostly modest success in identifying evolutionarily constrained elements in mammalian genomes. To address this issue, we introduce a novel framework for training gLMs that explicitly models nucleotide evolution on phylogenetic trees using multispecies whole-genome alignments. Our approach integrates an alignment into the loss function during training but does not require it for making predictions, thereby enhancing the model's applicability. We applied this framework to train PhyloGPN, a model that excels at predicting functionally disruptive variants from a single sequence alone and demonstrates strong transfer learning capabilities.
notes:
## Key Findings

- PhyloGPN is a genomic language model trained with phylogenetic tree-aware loss using multispecies whole-genome alignments, outperforming standard gLMs at identifying evolutionarily constrained elements.
- The alignment is used only during training (in the loss function), not at inference, making PhyloGPN practical for single-sequence variant effect prediction.
- Demonstrates strong transfer learning capabilities, suggesting the phylogenetically-informed representations generalize well to downstream genomic tasks.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Novel training framework that models nucleotide evolution on phylogenetic trees via multispecies whole-genome alignment integration into the loss.
- PhyloGPN architecture allows inference from a single sequence, decoupling the need for alignment data at prediction time.

title: A pangenome reference and population studies link structural variants with breeding traits in Gossypium hirsutum
abstract: Pangenome analyses of 28 representative Gossypium hirsutum accessions, including a telomere-to-telomere (T2T) genome for elite cultivar NDM13 and near-T2T genomes for 27 additional representatives spanning the recent century of breeding, with transcriptomic profiling of 15 distinct tissues from each. The study uncovered 51,551 one-to-one conserved orthologs and revealed hotspots of structural variation (SV) and impacts of SV, segmental duplication and copy number variants on gene expression and content alteration, as well as adversity resistances. The pangenome identified thousands of divergent SVs and genes implicated in modern breeding evolution. By combining T2T-reference-based pangenome construction and 761,536 SVs identified across 1,671 worldwide accessions with phenotypic data from 22 environments, the study captured structural variations associated with breeding traits in upland cotton.
notes:
## Key Findings

- T2T genome assembled for elite cotton cultivar NDM13 + near-T2T for 27 additional accessions spanning ~100 years of breeding — first comprehensive upland cotton pangenome.
- 761,536 SVs genotyped across 1,671 worldwide accessions; SV hotspots linked to gene expression, segmental duplications, and copy number variation.
- Population-scale GWAS using SVs + phenotypic data from 22 environments reveals structural variants causally associated with agronomic breeding traits.

## Methods Worth Noting

- T2T + near-T2T assembly pipeline for 28 allotetraploid cotton accessions.
- Transcriptomic profiling across 15 tissues per accession for expression QTL analysis.
- SV genotyping across 1,671 accessions for large-scale pangenome-GWAS.

title: Generalist biological artificial intelligence in modeling the language of life
abstract: Generalist biological artificial intelligence (GBAI) represents a transformative approach to modeling the 'language of life'—the flow of information from DNA to cellular function. This Review synthesizes rapid advances in biological AI to interpret and generate DNA, RNA, proteins and cellular systems, charting a course toward comprehensive systems that can concurrently process and predict across these domains, performing several critical biological tasks simultaneously. The Review discusses opportunities in synergizing language and structural AI, leveraging specialized models and improving AI agents for autonomous discovery, and presents a vision for generalist biological artificial intelligence in which models can perform diverse tasks across biological domains. GBAI has the potential to deepen understanding of disease pathways and biomarkers, advance automated therapeutic design and evaluation, and integrate within virtual cells to meaningfully simulate biological activity.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Defines GBAI (Generalist Biological AI) as the emerging paradigm where a single model spans DNA→RNA→protein→cell, performing diverse biological tasks concurrently.
- Reviews promises and pitfalls of current bio-AI algorithms: specialization vs. generalization trade-offs, data modality gaps, benchmarking challenges.
- Charts a roadmap toward virtual cell integration where GBAI models simulate biological activity end-to-end.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Review article synthesizing architectures across DNA LMs (Evo, Nucleotide Transformer), RNA models, protein LMs (ESM), and cell-level FMs (scGPT, scPRINT).
- Discusses multi-modal fusion strategies and autonomous AI agent frameworks for biological discovery.

title: A kingdom-wide full-length RNA atlas reveals the evolutionary landscapes of plant poly(A) tails
abstract: Background: Poly(A) tails play crucial roles in regulating mRNA degradation and translation, yet their evolutionary dynamics remain largely unexplored. Results: Here, we generate a comprehensive full-length RNA atlas of poly(A) tail based on 130 million nanopore long-read transcripts spanning major lineages from algae to angiosperms. We find lineage-specific tail-length profiles: most land plants display peaks at approximately 20 and 45 nt, whereas green algae show a distinct peak at approximately 40 nt. Unexpectedly, poly(A)-less histone mRNAs are detected in several plant lineages, but are independently lost multiple times and replaced by polyadenylated transcripts in Selaginella and all angiosperms. Across all species, terminal uridylation typically targets poly(A) tails shorter than 25 nt. Poly(A) tail lengths of whole genome duplication derived gene pairs diverge over time, yet certain genes maintain relatively conserved distributions across species, which underscores poly(A) length regulation as an important post-transcriptional regulatory layer. Conclusions: Our study provides the first comprehensive poly(A) tail dataset spanning all major lineages of plant evolution, uncovering lineage-specific poly(A) tail length distributions. Notably, we reveal the presence of poly(A)-less transcripts in several plant clades, which is overlooked in previous transcriptomic studies. This atlas offers a valuable resource for understanding the evolutionary and regulatory complexity of mRNA 3' end formation across the plant kingdom.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Generated a kingdom-wide poly(A) tail atlas from 130 million nanopore long-read transcripts spanning algae to angiosperms
- Land plants show bimodal tail-length peaks (~20 and ~45 nt); green algae peak at ~40 nt — lineage-specific profiles reveal evolutionary divergence
- Poly(A)-less histone mRNAs persist in some plant lineages but were independently replaced by polyadenylated forms in Selaginella and all angiosperms

## Methods Worth Noting

- Oxford Nanopore Technology (ONT) long-read direct RNA sequencing to measure exact poly(A) tail lengths at single-transcript resolution
- Cross-species comparative transcriptomics spanning major plant lineages (algae to angiosperms)
- Integration of WGD gene pairs to examine divergence of poly(A) tail lengths after polyploidy events

title: Dynamic and cell-type specific transcriptional reprogramming underlies the floral transition in the maize shoot apical meristem
abstract: Background: The floral transition in maize represents a pivotal developmental switch that determines flowering time, environmental adaptation, and yield-related traits. However, the molecular mechanisms governing shoot apical meristem reprogramming and cell identity changes during this process remain poorly understood. Results: By integrating time-course bulk RNA-seq, single-cell transcriptomics, chromatin accessibility, and transcription factor binding profiles, we construct a spatiotemporal molecular framework of the maize shoot apical meristem floral transition. Our analyses reveal global transcriptional reprogramming accompanied by pronounced cell type-specific regulation dynamics. At a global level, our transcriptional-level inference suggests that pathways associated with chromatin remodeling, environmental response, and reproductive development are sequentially activated. We further identify a ZmMADS69-ZmRap2.7-ZMM4 regulatory module that fine-tunes the floral transition within the shoot apical meristem. At single-cell resolution, we find that the floral transition is not driven by a uniform transcriptional switch, but instead emerges from the coordinated action of spatially distinct shoot apical meristem domains. Through differential expression, trajectory, and co-expression module analyses, we further identify previously unrecognized roles for the inflorescence regulators UNBRANCHED2 and UNBRANCHED3 in promoting the floral transition, suggesting that they coordinate floral induction with subsequent inflorescence development. Conclusions: Our study establishes a comprehensive spatiotemporal regulatory framework for the maize floral transition, providing mechanistic insights into shoot apical meristem reprogramming and offering a foundation for identifying new regulators to improve maize adaptation and yield.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Constructed a spatiotemporal single-cell atlas of maize shoot apical meristem (SAM) floral transition using multi-omic data
- Identified ZmMADS69-ZmRap2.7-ZMM4 as a key regulatory module fine-tuning floral timing within the SAM
- Discovered new roles for inflorescence regulators UNBRANCHED2 and UNBRANCHED3 in promoting floral transition, linking floral induction with inflorescence development

## Methods Worth Noting

- Time-course integration of bulk RNA-seq + scRNA-seq + ATAC-seq + ChIP-seq in maize SAM
- Single-cell trajectory and co-expression module analysis to infer regulatory dynamics across SAM domains
- Spatially distinct SAM domain analysis revealing cell-type-specific transcriptional programs during development

title: Using reciprocally retained gene families to detect whole-genome multiplications in plants
abstract: Background: Traces of ancient whole-genome multiplications (WGMs) have been observed all over the plant kingdom and have been associated with various evolutionary processes, such as increased evolvability, speciation, adaptation to changing environments, domestication and the origin of evolutionary novelties. However, understanding the impact of WGMs on plant evolution requires accurate detection of WGM events, which is challenging because of rapid signal erosion due to genome rearrangements, sequence divergence and the occurrence of additional large- and small-scale duplications. Results: Here, we investigate whether reciprocally retained gene families, i.e. gene families that preferentially expand through WGM and rarely exhibit small-scale duplications, can be used as WGM markers in angiosperms. Using stochastic birth-death modeling of gene families' gene count profiles across species to test for WGM presence or absence, we demonstrate that strongly reciprocally retained gene families have higher power to detect true WGMs and to reject false WGMs than non-reciprocally retained gene families. Conclusions: Although none of the reciprocally retained gene families is a perfect WGM marker on its own, we show that using an extended set of such families as input for birth-death and KS distribution modeling approaches leads to better WGM detection performance than using entire genomes. A pipeline to use reciprocally retained gene families for KS distribution-based WGM inference in angiosperms has been implemented in ksrates v2.0.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Reciprocally retained gene families (those that expand through WGM and rarely duplicate by other means) serve as higher-power markers for detecting ancient whole-genome multiplications in angiosperms
- Stochastic birth-death modeling on gene count profiles outperforms whole-genome approaches for both sensitivity and specificity in WGM detection
- Using a diverse extended set of such families as input improves WGM detection over using entire genomes

## Methods Worth Noting

- Stochastic birth-death modeling of gene family evolution across angiosperm species trees
- KS distribution modeling for dating WGM events
- Implemented in ksrates v2.0 pipeline — a practical tool for angiosperm comparative genomics

title: Mapping cis-regulatory mutations at scale in sorghum enables modulation of gene expression
abstract: Precise modulation of gene expression through cis-regulatory editing holds promise for nontransgenic crop improvement. However, the sequence-to-function relationships that govern plant promoter activity remain poorly understood. Here we develop a massively parallel reporter assay in Sorghum bicolor to systematically measure the effects of >30,000 mutations spanning deletions, substitutions and motif insertions accessible through CRISPR editing across entire native promoters and 5′ untranslated regions of 3 photosynthesis genes: PsbS, Raf1 and SBPase. We find that gene expression is most tunable within a ~500-bp core promoter region. The mutational effects are reproducible across biological replicates and predictive of protein output. Within these regions, we identify compact deletions and motif insertions that strongly increase protein production (>30-fold relative to wild type), outperforming transgenic enhancer elements. Mutation–effect relationships are gene specific, highlighting the need for tailored regulatory maps. Our results establish a high-throughput strategy for cis-regulatory fine-mapping that may enable crop improvements through minimal, precise and nontransgenic gene edits.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Developed a massively parallel reporter assay (MPRA) in sorghum to measure >30,000 cis-regulatory mutations in native promoters of 3 photosynthesis genes (PsbS, Raf1, SBPase)
- Gene expression is most tunable within a ~500-bp core promoter region; identified compact deletions and motif insertions that increase protein production >30-fold over wild type
- Mutation–effect relationships are gene-specific, establishing that tailored regulatory maps are needed for each target gene

## Methods Worth Noting

- Massively parallel reporter assay (MPRA) in Sorghum bicolor combining CRISPR-accessible mutations (deletions, substitutions, motif insertions)
- Functional mapping of entire native promoters and 5′ UTRs at scale; mutations assessed for reproducibility across biological replicates and correlation with protein output

title: Compressing the collective knowledge of ESM into a single protein language model
abstract: Protein language models (PLMs) have recently emerged as a promising approach for next-generation variant-effect prediction (VEP). Most high-performing VEP methods currently utilize PLMs combined with additional information, such as homology, protein structure and population genetics data to improve prediction accuracy. This performance gain, however, comes with added complexity or limited applicability compared to pure PLMs trained only on raw, unaligned sequences, such as evolutionary scale modeling (ESM). Here we challenge the prevailing view that sequence-only PLMs are intrinsically limited and present an efficient co-distillation approach to adapt them for high-accuracy VEP without requiring additional information beyond evolutionary signals captured during pretraining. We allow individual PLMs to self-improve by distilling the most confident predictions from multiple models of the same family and demonstrate that co-distillation of ESM models suffices to achieve state-of-the-art performance across multiple VEP benchmarks. We further show that this performance increase enables accurate quantification of the severity of variant effects on continuous clinical phenotypes in biobank data.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Co-distillation of ESM protein language models achieves state-of-the-art variant effect prediction (VEP) without requiring structural, homology, or population genetics data beyond the evolutionary signals in pretraining
- Individual PLMs self-improve by distilling the most confident predictions from multiple models in the same family, enabling iterative performance gains
- The improved VEP accuracy enables quantification of variant effect severity on continuous clinical phenotypes in biobank data

## Methods Worth Noting

- Co-distillation framework applied to ESM family models: iterative adaptation using only sequence data
- Benchmarked across multiple VEP datasets; compared against methods using additional structural/homology information

title: An evolutionary landscape of sesame: chromosomal variation, allopolyploid speciation and metabolic specialization
abstract: Sesame (Sesamum indicum) is one of the earliest domesticated oilseed crops and is valued for antioxidant lignans that stabilize oil quality. However, the genomic and evolutionary history of the genus Sesamum, including the origin of its allotetraploid relative S. radiatum and the diversification of lignan metabolism, remains poorly understood owing to limited chromosome-scale genomic resources. Here we present chromosome-level genome assemblies for three wild Sesamum species, two Ceratotheca species and a Japanese sesame cultivar to reconstruct genome and karyotype evolution across the Sesamum-Ceratotheca complex. Comparative analyses show that the derived x=16 lineage originated from an ancestral x=13 karyotype through chromosome fission, fusion and translocation, whereas another x=13 lineage underwent extensive restructuring associated with retrotransposon expansion. Phylogenomics places Ceratotheca within the x=16 Sesamum clade and reveals that S. radiatum originated through hybridization involving a C. sesamoides-like ancestor. The antioxidative lignan gene CYP92B14 was reintroduced via the BB progenitor, linking hybridization with restoration of oil-stabilizing metabolism during sesame evolution.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Chromosome-level assemblies for 6 Sesamum/Ceratotheca species: x=16 karyotype derived from x=13 via chromosome fission, fusion, and translocation.
- S. radiatum (allotetraploid) originated through hybridization with a C. sesamoides-like ancestor; antioxidant lignan gene CYP92B14 reintroduced via BB progenitor.
- Retrotransposon expansion associated with extensive genome restructuring in a second x=13 lineage.

## Methods Worth Noting

- Chromosome-level genome assembly for 6 species (3 wild Sesamum, 2 Ceratotheca, 1 Japanese cultivar)
- Phylogenomics to reconstruct karyotype and WGD history across Sesamum-Ceratotheca complex
- Comparative transcriptomics to trace lignan biosynthesis gene (CYP92B14) evolution

title: Haplotype-resolved Genome Assemblies of Hybrid Wheatgrass and Bluebunch Wheatgrass Reveal the Stepwise Polyploid Origin and Biased Subgenome Dominance
abstract: Concerns over climate change have intensified the demand for stress resistant crops like hybrid wheatgrass (HWG; Elymus hoffmannii, StStStStHH), a perennial forage species known for its exceptional salt and drought tolerance. However, hexaploidy and high heterozygosity have complicated efforts to resolve its genomic structure and evolutionary history. Here, we present high-quality, haplotype-resolved, chromosome-level genome assemblies for HWG (CDC Saltking) and its putative progenitor, bluebunch wheatgrass (Pseudoroegneria spicata). By integrating PacBio HiFi and ultra-long Oxford Nanopore sequencing with Hi-C scaffolding, we assembled the 10.7 Gb HWG genome into 21 pseudochromosomes per haplotype. Our phylogenomic analysis redefines the origin of the H subgenome, positioning it as an intermediate between Old-World Hordeum marinum (sea barley) and Hordeum brevisubulatum. Notably, we identified significant chromosomal rearrangements, including a unique duplication on St chromosome 4. Transcriptome analysis across multiple tissues revealed a pronounced expression dominance of the H subgenome. This dominance was not associated with reduced LTR density, suggesting that selective pressures for rapid adaptation of the latest subgenome entrant may drive its dominance. Using the f-branch statistic, population genomic analysis of 189 accessions representing eight Elymus and Pseudoroegneria species revealed extensive reticulate evolutionary relationships and identified P. spicata as a major, asymmetric genetic donor within the wheatgrass complex.
notes:
## Key Findings

- First haplotype-resolved chromosome-level assembly of hexaploid hybrid wheatgrass (10.7 Gb, 21 pseudochromosomes per haplotype) and diploid progenitor P. spicata.
- H subgenome origin repositioned between Old-World Hordeum marinum and H. brevisubulatum; H subgenome shows pronounced expression dominance not explained by LTR density.
- Population genomics (189 accessions) reveals P. spicata as major asymmetric genetic donor; extensive reticulate evolution in Elymus/Pseudoroegneria complex.

## Methods Worth Noting

- PacBio HiFi + ultra-long ONT + Hi-C scaffolding for hexaploid haplotype assembly
- f-branch statistic for detecting introgression in polyploid lineages
- Subgenome homeolog expression bias analysis across multiple tissues

title: A Pangenome Centralized NLRome Drives Lineage Specific Diversification and Functional Differentiation in Solanoideae
abstract: The Solanoideae subfamily comprises numerous economically important crops that deploy a diverse repertoire of nucleotide-binding leucine-rich repeat receptors (NLRs) to defend against pathogens. However, the evolutionary trajectory of these NLR immune receptors remains poorly understood. Here, we dissect NLR family evolution across 23 Solanoideae species by integrating genomic and transcriptomic data. We uncover a distinctive pangenome-centralized architecture wherein a mere 11.56% of soft-core orthogroups contribute 67.81% of the total NLR repertoire, with copy-number variation driven by lineage-specific expansions rather than stochastic distribution or genome size. This finding indicates that Solanoideae plants counteract biotic stress through targeted amplification of specific key families rather than undergoing broad diversification. Phylogenomic analysis resolves NLRs into four subclasses, revealing CC-NLR dominance across all species alongside extreme TIR-NLR degeneration in certain lineages, suggesting profound immune adaptive shifts. Mechanistically, recent NLR expansions are primarily fueled by proximal and tandem duplications, whereas whole-genome duplications contribute in a ploidy- and time-dependent manner, with WGD-derived genes undergoing extensive loss during diploidization. Transcriptional profiling further demonstrates duplication-type-dependent expression patterns, enabling a robust, multilayered defense network upon pathogen infection. Collectively, our study delineates the asymmetric evolutionary trajectory of the Solanoideae NLRome and establishes a framework model that provides new insights into the adaptive evolution of plant immune systems and resistance gene discovery.
notes:
## Key Findings

- Pan-NLRome of 23 Solanoideae species: 11.56% of soft-core orthogroups encode 67.81% of all NLRs — a highly concentrated immune architecture driven by targeted expansion.
